author: @petevenuti7355
text:
Have you, or have any of your videos covered, ionic liquid based thermoelectric batteries? Apparently some melted salts and other ionic combinations have very a strong seabeck/peltier effect! One of these ions happens to be cyanoferate. 
   I mentioned some ideas about this to another YouTuber I believe you know, that linked me to this video, cayrex2 , thinking it might have applications with his zinc hexacyanoferate battery!

   Please let me know if you can investigate or pursue some research into this as I would love to see such chemistry open sourced before it could get locked down by commercial interest!

Oh , please don't forget to link to the papers that you reference in the description or comments , I find that often I can't find them or sometimes it takes a lot of time even if I can.. 
..but I do understand sometimes it's a vague memory or your computer crashes, I mean I would love to give you a reference about cyanoferete thermal electric batteries, or at least find so I can learn how to spell it but if you can't spell it how do you find?🤷

author: @travismoore7849
text: Ever try to make nano particles of zinc by using zinc powder dissolving in methanol and pva stirred by a low speed mixer like you use for cooking? I managed to hollow out pennies with 91% rubbing alcohol. Now Zinc sulfur batteries can be made using a metal fuel cell if you a solid state ion exchange membrane or catalyst doped carbon paper. With sulfur water or ammonium sulfate electrolyzed acting as the oxidizer. The ammonium sulfate may be worked with two jars and a salt bridge and charging it. Though carbon strings made of carbon nano tubes may be better than graphite and zinc electrode. You can mix methanol or alcohol in zinc cells to reduce dendrites.

author: @mrdovie47
text: I got a small battery FM transmitter that takes a microphone or line input. This is used near, or better yet, worn by the one speaking with a small broadcast FM receiver tuned to a dead spot on FM. the receiver sends the sound to the phone or whatever is recording the video. (In my case a PC with a line or mic. input) This proves better than trying to shout to a distant microphone. DSP PLL 87-108MHz Multi-function FM Stereo Transmitter Module / Microphone Wireless Transmitter / Wireless Microphone Module is what to look for. I use one of these for my PC audio also running off a USB power and I can pick up my audio anywhere in the house on an FM receiver.

author: @RWBHere
text:
Why are people complaining? I can hear it, even on an old iPad. Robert has been generous in releasing this video, _free of charge_ (no pun intended), so that anyone with a modicum of gumption and an active mind can learn some ideas and techniques from it. Even those who 'cannot afford' a kit can benefit from it, by improvising, and experimenting with any number of combinations of electrolytes, separators and electrodes, not to mention variations in construction, temperatures, processes, etc.,  which Robert could never have enough time to test. Who knows, with the right attitude, and some time and elbow grease, _you_ could be the one who discovers the next step in the evolution of cell and battery design. 

There's absolutely no need for anyone to complain, even if the audio would fail the entrance exam, or if the video had been filmed with a potato. These people are not professional sound and video engineers, but they have enough passion for their researches, and care enough about what others might be able to accomplish, to share some of what they know with anyone who cares to persevere with the very interesting and useful live video presentation.

I ask myself, 'Could I have made a better video under the circumstances?'. My answer would very likely be, 'No!'. 

Why not reflect their kindness by trying out the methods, and by sharing _your_ results and findings with others?

replies.author: @RWBHere
replies.text: P.S. I've downloaded the video, to watch in full, offline, later. Thank-you, to all concerned.

author: @johanbrand2443
text:
Some additional information to the summary by  Flashlit123:  I hope it all sounds correct.

Mix 0.5 grams Potassium-Hexacyanoferrate (bag #1 in kit) with 100 ml Distilled water.  In separate container, mix 0.5 grams Zinc Sulphate (bag #2 in kit) in 100 ml Distilled water.  While stirring, very slowly (drop by drop) mix these two solutions to form nano particles of orange precipitate of Zinc-Hexacyanoferrate.  [Mixing time by hand took about 30 to 40 minutes.]
[Centrifuge the orange solution or wait for it to settle and separate (banding).]  
[Filter the solution using a coffee type paper filter. Allow  the top band to settle in the filter paper first to reduce the pore size before adding the rest is helpful but not necessary.][Filter time can take several hours.]
[save the orange material and drying the precipitate as your active material. The rest can be discarded.] [Drying time with hair dryer took about 5 minutes.]

Make a paint in a mortar and pestle containing by weight:
70% active material (the precipitated powder you just made ground to fine powder) [0.5 grams]
20% carbon or graphite (bag #4 in kit) or Carbon-Black as conductive material. [0.15 grams]
10% binder CMC/SBR (container #3 in kit)   [0.06 grams or 4 drops]
[CMC is carboxymethyl cellulose and SBR is styrenebutyl rubber a.k.a. Styrene-butadiene latex (?a builders type of water seal cement  additive?)]
[you can a little bit of Distilled water if  the paste is to dry]
[Should look like a brownish paste / smear]

Paint/smear this mixture on one side of the Grafoil* electrode (positive cathode) and let dry. (there should be enough for a 1cm x 1cm size.)  [Drying time with hair dryer took about 5 minutes.]
[Thick layer => power dense and less energy dense; Thin layer => Energy Dense and less power dense.] 
*{GRAFOIL® is a Flexible Graphite Foil sealing material made exclusively from pure, natural graphite flake for sealing solution applicable for the automotive gasket market.} 
[Alternative: use fine 400 mesh Stainless Steel 316 strips] 
[Alternative: Make your own conductive paper  like this...  https://youtu.be/dFrpoRzdINA ]

No coating for the zinc electrode (negative anode).

To make the cell you need four components:
1 - the 1 cm^2 Cathode made of graphite paper and covered by Zinc-Hexacyanoferrate
2 - the 1 cm^2 Anode - Zinc metal strip
2 - 3 ml Electrolyte (see below)
4 - a separation Membrane

Make 1 molar solution of the zinc sulphate from bag #2 of the kit for the electrolyte.  Assuming it is the heptahydrate, this should take about 287 grams per liter or 0.287 grams per ml.  Very little solution is needed [about - 3 ml].

Assemble the cell separating the electrodes with the Celgard sheet included in the kit and add the electrolyte to dampen the separator. 
Celgard is provided and is a polypropylene microporous membrane made from nonwoven fibers (cotton, nylon, polyesters, glass) and polymer films (polyethylene, polypropylene, etc).  [This material is a tri-layer of polyethylene, polypropylene, polyethylene where the outer layer is coated with ZirconiumOxide to prevent Zinc ion migration.  
[Alternative: you can use a self-made membrane made from tissue paper and painted on the anode side with a water based ZirconiumOxide coating or also available in spry Aerosol can.]

Weight or clamp it together [or place this in a small plastic polypropylene bag that will keep everything together and vacuum seal.]
Charge it up [~1.8V] by connecting the Grafoil to the positive and the Zinc to the negative, and test it out.

author: @handleismyhandle
text: Proton (hydrogen ion) batteries seem very promising. . . I came upon the idea while reading about nickel metal hydride batteries and lamenting the high weight of bulk nickel and lanthanum. It gave me the idea of pre-loading a pyrolitic carbon foam with hydrogen, and using it in combination with a reversible combining agent like manganese dioxide or something. A rechargeable water/carbon cell, is further thinking along these lines, basically, and there are even non-precious catalysts that can serve in place of the platinum that is normally used in such a device. Maybe graphitic carbon nitride could be seeded with nickel or something else to make a room temperature, low pressure catalyst at the reaction interface. Not sure about all of the possible routes to success for this sort of design, but various carbons will intercalate large amouts of hydrogen, some under  normal temperature and pressure conditions. The potential energy density would be enormous, and eventually somebody will figure out (or has figured out) how to do all this without any precious or overly engineered materials. I'll download this video to watch at home.

author: @JehuMcSpooran
text:
Loved the live stream Rob. I watched later and found it very informative. I do have a few questions. 
1. I'm assuming that if you change the HCFe nanoparticle compound to a different metal you would need to use a different anode material, i.e. a copper or aluminium sulfate to the KHCFe mix would make a CuHCFe or AlHCFe nanoparticle active material and therefore require a copper or aluminium anode? 
2. A few people mentioned Prussian Blue. From what I have read, this doesn't actually dissolve but is a colloidal in water. Would this need to be reacted with zinc sulfate to make the ZnHCFe or can it be used by itself with an iron anode or even a stainless steel anode? 
3. What is the ratio of CMC to SBR before we add that to the rest of the mix if we are making it from scratch?


Thanks again Rob

author: @albertmueller6616
text: Agreed, lots of negativity over sound however think if you had to pay enough for the kit to enable Rob to be able to bring in professional PPL to broadcast this. I for one could not have afforded that. He will improve these live broadcasts provided he isn't scared off from doing another by all the negative comments. Besides, who is the first to say they haven't gotten $50.00 ( price of kit ) worth of education from his video's . Actually the first thing you get from this one alone is the recipe and technique for making Zink Hexacyanoferrate and that alone is worth the price of the kit as you cant buy it anyway. If you didn't buy the kit you got no room to complain and those who did realize this is geared towards teaching something they need. The rest already know what this one teaches and don't care about the sound anyway. Cheapest education I have ever gotten I believe. More bang for the buck here than in most all you-tube video's. Its a simple matter of the acoustic properties of an industrial unit, like marinerewable said. Its a big ole room. Simply hanging a sheet or tarp or something overhead would fix that. I was able to make a viable battery for the first time because of this and that is what its geared towards. I am grateful and would do the next one if you continue these Rob. Try, Try again. THANKS ROB !!!

author: @dekutree64
text: Binder questions: At 2:02:40 you say a drop is .015ml, but according to the internet it's .060ml per drop, and according to my own counting drops of water from the pipette, it's .044ml per drop. But even 4 drops was barely enough to wet the active material. It is 10% wet binder weight, right? Not 10% dry binder in the finished cell? Next time should I do 2 drops, and add water until it's wet enough to mix and spread?

author: @dekutree64
text:
I can't believe it, my battery actually works!

The most unexpectedly difficult part was getting the active material to spread somewhat evenly on the grafoil. Darn stuff wants to stick to everything else... Then I think I put too much electrolyte on it because weighting it down squeezed some of its guts out. But it charged up just fine and spun the propeller for a few minutes. Then I went to seal it in packing tape, but half way peeled it apart while trying to clean up some of the squeezed out active material. Got it back together, taped up, and it still works. It's been sitting at 1.8V for a few hours now, so not even self discharging despite the messiness.

Thank you Rob! Always the hardest part of learning something new is getting from no result to something half way functional.

author: @flashlit123
text:
Mix 0.5 grams Potassium Hexacyanoferrate (bag #1 in kit) with 100 ml water.  In separate container, mix 0.5 grams Zinc Sulphate (bag #2 in kit) in 100 ml water.  While stirring, very slowly (drop by drop) mix these two solutions to form nano particles.  Filter the solution saving and drying the precipitate as your active material.

Make a paint containing by weight:
70% active material (the precipitated powder you just made)
20% carbon or graphite (bag #4 in kit)
10% binder CMC/SBR (container #3 in kit)

Paint this mixture on the grafoil electrode (positive cathode) and let dry.  No coating for the zinc electrode (negative anode).

Make 1 molar solution of the zinc sulphate from bag #2 of the kit for the electrolyte.  Assuming it is the heptahydrate, this should take about 287 grams per liter or 0.287 grams per ml.  Very little solution is needed.

Assemble the cell separating the electrodes with the celgard sheet included in the kit and add the electrolyte to dampen the separator.  Weight or clamp it together, charge it up, and test it out.

author: @patricksweetman3285
text: The copper analogue of prussian blue would be easily made using the readily available CuSO4 instead of ZnSO4, but would that necessitate using a copper based electrolyte and a copper metal anode?

replies.author: @patricksweetman3285
replies.text: To reply to my own post.  No, the battery is a zinc ion battery, analagous to a lithium ion battery, so the electrolyte has to be a zinc salt and the electrode has to be zinc to provide more zinc ions.  Copper ions would probably not fit into the crystal structure of the precipitate, but it might be a fun experiment to find out.

author: @EmmittBrownBTTF1
text: @Kapale84 your battery is likely over discharged, if charged for 5 seconds by a fully charged battery, it may hit a voltage high enough for the charger to charge it. The charger may be rejecting it because the voltage is too low. There is a risk of dendrite formation and impressive failure. Use a microwave oven shell outside and don't over charge. remember the charging battery may be stressed by high current draw.

author: @benthere8051
text: You need to line your ceiling with egg crates. The acoustics are terrible.

replies.author: @vlogsaboutnothing1480
replies.text: That'll only act as a diffuser and a poor one at the most. They need absorption in the room, a proper microphone, and a better sampling rate for the streaming.
